name: Compile Rust to WebAssembly with wasm-pack
description: Build Rust libraries as WebAssembly modules for use in browsers and Node.js with wasm-pack and wasm-bindgen.
trendingReason: Rust-to-Wasm is the leading path for high-performance browser computation

steps.title: Install wasm-pack
steps.description: Install the tool that builds, tests, and publishes Rust-generated WebAssembly.

steps.title: Create a new wasm library project
steps.description: Initialize a library crate configured for WebAssembly output.
steps.command: cargo new --lib my-wasm-lib && cd my-wasm-lib && cargo add wasm-bindgen

steps.title: Configure the crate type
steps.description: Set the crate type to cdylib in Cargo.toml for WebAssembly compilation.

steps.title: Write a wasm-bindgen function
steps.description: Create an exported function that can be called from JavaScript.

steps.title: Build the WebAssembly package
steps.description: Compile the library to WebAssembly and generate JavaScript bindings.
steps.tip: Use --target bundler for webpack/Vite projects, --target nodejs for Node.js, or --target web for direct browser use.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Rust to WebAssembly Build
set -euo pipefail

TARGET=${1:-"web"}

echo "=== Checking wasm-pack ==="
wasm-pack --version || { echo "Install with: cargo install wasm-pack"; exit 1; }

echo ""
echo "=== Building for target: $TARGET ==="
wasm-pack build --target "$TARGET" --release

echo ""
echo "=== Output Files ==="
ls -lh pkg/

echo ""
echo "=== Package Contents ==="
ls pkg/*.wasm pkg/*.js pkg/*.ts 2>/dev/null || true

echo ""
echo "=== wasm-pack Commands ==="
echo "Build (web):     wasm-pack build --target web"
echo "Build (bundler): wasm-pack build --target bundler"
echo "Build (node):    wasm-pack build --target nodejs"
echo "Test (browser):  wasm-pack test --chrome --headless"
echo "Test (node):     wasm-pack test --node"
echo "Publish:         wasm-pack publish"

faq.question: What is the difference between the build targets?
faq.answer: Use 'web' for ES modules loaded directly in browsers. Use 'bundler' for webpack or Vite projects. Use 'nodejs' for server-side Node.js usage. Each generates different JavaScript glue code.

faq.question: How large are Rust Wasm binaries?
faq.answer: A minimal function compiles to around 20-50KB of Wasm. Use wasm-opt (included in wasm-pack) and the release profile with opt-level 'z' to minimize size. The wee_alloc allocator can save additional kilobytes.

faq.question: Can I use async/await in Wasm?
faq.answer: Yes. wasm-bindgen supports async functions that return JavaScript Promises. Use wasm-bindgen-futures to bridge Rust futures with JavaScript Promises.
